content.text: Burst Scaling Strategies for Agentic AI Systems

Agentic workloads have a fundamentally different demand profile than traditional stateless web services. A single user action can cascade into dozens of tool calls, each spawning its own LLM request. End-of-sprint batch runs, product launch promotions, and scheduled report generation all create demand spikes that arrive faster than any human operator can respond. Effective burst scaling requires characterising the spike shape, pre-warming standby capacity, choosing the right autoscaler signal, and designing the system to absorb temporary overload without data loss.

attrs.title: Autoscaler consuming queue depth and P95 latency metrics to drive scale-out, scale-in, and pre-warmed standby decisions

content.text: Burst Characterisation
Demand Spike vs Gradual Ramp
Not all bursts are the same. Correct diagnosis drives the right mitigation:
Demand spike

— a near-instantaneous jump in request rate caused by an external event: a mass email send, a cron job triggering overnight batch work, or a viral social media moment. Spikes peak within seconds to minutes and require a pre-warming strategy because the autoscaler cannot provision infrastructure fast enough.
Gradual ramp

— usage grows steadily over hours or days as a new feature is rolled out or the user base grows. Gradual ramps respond well to reactive autoscaling because the lag between metric crossing a threshold and new capacity being ready (typically 60–120 seconds for container startup) is absorbed by the slow approach to saturation.
Bursty batch

— a scheduler fires N jobs simultaneously (end-of-day report generation, nightly data summarisation). These produce a square-wave demand profile: zero for hours, then maximum for a fixed duration, then zero again. Pre-warming timed to the schedule start is the optimal strategy.
Spike Shape Metrics
Measure three parameters from historical traces to characterise your bursts:
Rise time
— the interval from baseline to peak. Less than 2 minutes classifies as a spike and requires pre-warming or standby capacity.
Peak duration
— how long the workload remains at peak before beginning to decline. Peaks shorter than the autoscaler's scale-up latency mandate pre-provisioning.
Peak-to-baseline ratio
— peak requests per minute divided by the 7-day P50 baseline. Ratios above 5× usually exceed what a single tier upgrade can absorb and require a combination of scaling and request queuing.
Pre-Warming Strategies
Scheduled Pre-Warming

When burst timing is predictable (nightly batch at 02:00, Monday morning surge), scale out proactively 5–10 minutes before the expected start time using a scheduled HPA override or a Kubernetes CronJob that patches the
minReplicas
field:

import subprocess
from datetime import datetime, time as dt_time
import schedule
import time


def prewarm_agent_workers(min_replicas: int = 10) -> None:
    subprocess.run([
        "kubectl", "patch", "hpa", "agent-worker-hpa",
        "-n", "agentic-platform",
        "--type", "merge",
        "-p", f'{{"spec":{{"minReplicas":{min_replicas}}}}}',
    ], check=True)
    print(f"[{datetime.now().isoformat()}] Pre-warmed to {min_replicas} replicas")


def restore_min_replicas(min_replicas: int = 2) -> None:
    subprocess.run([
        "kubectl", "patch", "hpa", "agent-worker-hpa",
        "-n", "agentic-platform",
        "--type", "merge",
        "-p", f'{{"spec":{{"minReplicas":{min_replicas}}}}}',
    ], check=True)
    print(f"[{datetime.now().isoformat()}] Restored minReplicas to {min_replicas}")


schedule.every().day.at("01:50").do(prewarm_agent_workers, min_replicas=10)
schedule.every().day.at("04:00").do(restore_min_replicas, min_replicas=2)

while True:
    schedule.run_pending()
    time.sleep(30)
Standby Pod Pool

Maintain a small pool of pre-initialised pods that hold a loaded model client and a pre-authenticated LLM connection. Standby pods consume minimal CPU but respond to the first request within milliseconds rather than the 30–60 seconds required to boot a cold pod, pull credentials, and establish a connection. Size the pool at 20% of your P95 peak replica count or a minimum of 2 pods, whichever is larger.
Horizontal vs Vertical Scaling Decisions
When Horizontal Scaling Wins

Horizontal scaling (adding pod replicas) is almost always correct for stateless agent workers. The agent processes requests independently; there is no shared mutable state that makes multi-instance coordination complex. Benefits:
Linear throughput scaling: doubling replicas roughly doubles RPM capacity.
Fault isolation: a pod crash loses only the requests it was processing, not all in-flight work.
Incremental cost: scale in during troughs to pay only for active capacity.
Provider rate limit friendliness: multiple smaller workers each contribute to the shared RPM budget, requiring explicit quota management.
When Vertical Scaling Helps
Vertical scaling (larger instance type) is beneficial when:
Memory bottleneck
— a self-hosted embedding model or vector store co-located with the agent requires large RAM that cannot be split across nodes.
Inter-process communication overhead
— if the agent requires high-bandwidth communication with a GPU inference server on the same node, larger instances reduce network latency.
Licence constraints
— some commercial tool integrations are licensed per-host rather than per-container, making fewer larger nodes cheaper.

In practice, most agentic systems benefit from horizontal-first scaling up to the provider RPM limit, then vertical scaling or multi-account sharding if demand exceeds what a single provider tier can supply.
Autoscaler Tuning for AI Workloads
Why Standard CPU/Memory Metrics Fail
CPU and memory utilisation are poor proxies for LLM workload. An agent pod waiting for a streaming response is idle on CPU but occupying an expensive token budget. The relevant signals are:
Queue depth
— the number of pending requests per pod. This directly encodes processing backlog and maps cleanly to the number of additional pods needed.
P95 request latency

— the 95th-percentile end-to-end latency from request admission to response delivery. A rising P95 indicates the system is saturated even if queue depth appears low (e.g., because requests are being rejected before queuing).
Token throughput rate
— tokens processed per second across all workers; useful as a secondary signal when the primary constraint is provider TPM rather than pod concurrency.
Cooldown Asymmetry
Apply zero cooldown on scale-up and a 60-second cooldown on scale-down. The reasoning:
Scale-up delay causes immediate request failures or latency spikes; the cost of over-scaling for one cooldown window is much lower than the cost of user-visible degradation.
Scale-down too quickly during a brief dip re-triggers scale-up, creating an oscillation (thrash) that burns cloud costs and causes repeated cold-start delays.
The stabilisation window on scale-down should be at least 30 seconds (the typical duration of a brief inter-batch gap in bursty workloads).
Python Autoscaler Simulation

from __future__ import annotations
import math
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable


@dataclass
class AutoscalerConfig:
    min_replicas: int = 2
    max_replicas: int = 20
    target_queue_depth_per_replica: int = 10
    target_p95_latency_ms: float = 1_500
    scale_up_cooldown_s: float = 0.0    # scale up immediately
    scale_down_cooldown_s: float = 60.0
    stabilisation_window_s: float = 30.0
    prewarmed_standby: int = 2          # always-on pods in warm pool


@dataclass
class Metrics:
    queue_depth: int
    p95_latency_ms: float
    timestamp: float = field(default_factory=time.time)


class Autoscaler:
    def __init__(self, config: AutoscalerConfig):
        self.cfg = config
        self.current_replicas: int = config.min_replicas
        self._last_scale_up: float = 0.0
        self._last_scale_down: float = 0.0
        self._recent_metrics: list[Metrics] = []

    def _desired_from_queue(self, queue_depth: int) -> int:
        return math.ceil(queue_depth / self.cfg.target_queue_depth_per_replica)

    def _desired_from_latency(self, p95_ms: float) -> int:
        if p95_ms <= self.cfg.target_p95_latency_ms:
            return self.current_replicas
        ratio = p95_ms / self.cfg.target_p95_latency_ms
        return math.ceil(self.current_replicas * ratio)

    def _clamp(self, desired: int) -> int:
        return max(self.cfg.min_replicas, min(self.cfg.max_replicas, desired))

    def evaluate(self, metrics: Metrics) -> dict:
        now = metrics.timestamp
        self._recent_metrics = [m for m in self._recent_metrics if now - m.timestamp < self.cfg.stabilisation_window_s]
        self._recent_metrics.append(metrics)

        desired_q = self._desired_from_queue(metrics.queue_depth)
        desired_l = self._desired_from_latency(metrics.p95_latency_ms)
        desired = max(desired_q, desired_l)
        desired = self._clamp(desired)

        action = "none"
        if desired > self.current_replicas:
            if now - self._last_scale_up >= self.cfg.scale_up_cooldown_s:
                self._last_scale_up = now
                self.current_replicas = desired
                action = "scale_up"
        elif desired < self.current_replicas:
            if now - self._last_scale_down >= self.cfg.scale_down_cooldown_s:
                max_recent_desired = max(self._desired_from_queue(m.queue_depth) for m in self._recent_metrics)
                if max_recent_desired < self.current_replicas:
                    self._last_scale_down = now
                    self.current_replicas = max(desired, self.cfg.min_replicas)
                    action = "scale_down"

        return {
            "action": action,
            "current_replicas": self.current_replicas,
            "desired": desired,
            "queue_depth": metrics.queue_depth,
            "p95_latency_ms": metrics.p95_latency_ms,
        }


def simulate_burst(
    burst_queue_depths: list[int],
    p95_latencies: list[float],
    config: AutoscalerConfig | None = None,
) -> list[dict]:
    cfg = config or AutoscalerConfig()
    scaler = Autoscaler(cfg)
    results = []
    base_time = time.time()
    for tick, (depth, latency) in enumerate(zip(burst_queue_depths, p95_latencies)):
        m = Metrics(queue_depth=depth, p95_latency_ms=latency, timestamp=base_time + tick * 10)
        result = scaler.evaluate(m)
        result["tick"] = tick
        results.append(result)
    return results


if __name__ == "__main__":
    burst_depths = [5, 8, 30, 80, 150, 200, 180, 120, 60, 20, 8, 5]
    burst_latencies = [600, 700, 900, 1800, 3000, 3500, 3000, 2200, 1400, 900, 700, 600]

    results = simulate_burst(burst_depths, burst_latencies)
    print(f"{'Tick':>4} {'Action':<12} {'Replicas':>8} {'Q Depth':>8} {'P95 ms':>8}")
    print("-" * 48)
    for r in results:
        print(f"{r['tick']:>4} {r['action']:<12} {r['current_replicas']:>8} {r['queue_depth']:>8} {r['p95_latency_ms']:>8.0f}")
YAML HPA Configuration for Kubernetes

# hpa-agent-workers.yaml
# Kubernetes HPA tuned for agentic AI workloads (latency + queue-depth driven)
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: agent-worker-hpa
  namespace: agentic-platform
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: agent-worker
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 20

  metrics:
    # Primary signal: KEDA-exposed Redis queue depth
    - type: External
      external:
        metric:
          name: redis_stream_pending_messages
          selector:
            matchLabels:
              queue: agent-work-queue
        target:
          type: AverageValue
          averageValue: "10"    # target 10 pending per replica

    # Secondary signal: P95 request latency from Prometheus
    - type: External
      external:
        metric:
          name: http_request_duration_seconds_p95
          selector:
            matchLabels:
              service: agent-worker
        target:
          type: Value
          value: "1.5"          # 1.5 s threshold

  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0      # react immediately to burst
      policies:
        - type: Pods
          value: 4                       # add up to 4 pods per interval
          periodSeconds: 15
        - type: Percent
          value: 100                     # or double replicas per interval
          periodSeconds: 15
      selectPolicy: Max                  # use the more aggressive policy

    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 60     # wait 60 s before scaling in
      policies:
        - type: Pods
          value: 2
          periodSeconds: 60
      selectPolicy: Min                  # conservative scale-in

---
# keda-scaledobject.yaml — alternative for KEDA-only deployments
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: agent-worker-keda
  namespace: agentic-platform
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: agent-worker
  minReplicaCount: 2
  maxReplicaCount: 20
  cooldownPeriod: 60           # seconds after last scale event before scaling in
  triggers:
    - type: redis-streams
      metadata:
        address: redis:6379
        stream: agent-work-queue
        consumerGroup: agent-workers
        pendingEntriesCount: "10"   # scale when pending/replica > 10

    - type: prometheus
      metadata:
        serverAddress: http://prometheus:9090
        metricName: agent_p95_latency_seconds
        query: histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(agent_request_duration_seconds_bucket[2m]))
        threshold: "1.5"
Cost-of-Burst Analysis
The Two-Component Model
Burst cost has two independent components that must be budgeted separately:
Compute overage
— the incremental cost of extra pod replicas during the burst period. Compute overages are typically predictable and bounded.
Token overage

— if the burst drives the account above its committed monthly token tier, each additional token may be priced at a higher on-demand rate. Token overages can dwarf compute costs for LLM-heavy workloads.

from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class BurstCostModel:
    baseline_replicas: int
    instance_cost_usd_per_hour: float
    committed_tokens_per_month: int
    committed_token_price_per_mtok: float  # USD / million tokens
    overage_token_price_per_mtok: float    # USD / million tokens (on-demand)

    def compute_cost(
        self,
        burst_replicas: int,
        burst_duration_hours: float,
    ) -> float:
        extra_replicas = max(0, burst_replicas - self.baseline_replicas)
        return extra_replicas * self.instance_cost_usd_per_hour * burst_duration_hours

    def token_overage_cost(
        self,
        tokens_in_burst: int,
        tokens_used_before_burst: int,
    ) -> float:
        baseline_remaining = max(0, self.committed_tokens_per_month - tokens_used_before_burst)
        overage_tokens = max(0, tokens_in_burst - baseline_remaining)
        return overage_tokens / 1_000_000 * self.overage_token_price_per_mtok

    def total_burst_cost(
        self,
        burst_replicas: int,
        burst_duration_hours: float,
        tokens_in_burst: int,
        tokens_used_before_burst: int,
    ) -> dict:
        compute = self.compute_cost(burst_replicas, burst_duration_hours)
        token = self.token_overage_cost(tokens_in_burst, tokens_used_before_burst)
        return {
            "compute_overage_usd": round(compute, 2),
            "token_overage_usd": round(token, 2),
            "total_usd": round(compute + token, 2),
        }


model = BurstCostModel(
    baseline_replicas=2,
    instance_cost_usd_per_hour=0.15,
    committed_tokens_per_month=50_000_000,
    committed_token_price_per_mtok=3.0,
    overage_token_price_per_mtok=4.5,
)
print(model.total_burst_cost(
    burst_replicas=10,
    burst_duration_hours=2,
    tokens_in_burst=5_000_000,
    tokens_used_before_burst=47_000_000,
))
# {"compute_overage_usd": 2.4, "token_overage_usd": 13.5, "total_usd": 15.9}
Optimising Burst Cost
Pre-purchase burst capacity
— many providers offer committed burst token packages at 20–30% below on-demand rates. Buy burst capacity for known recurring events.
Model tiering during bursts
— route lower-priority burst requests to smaller, cheaper models. The token price advantage can exceed 5× with minimal quality impact for classification or summarisation tasks.
Queue-first, scale-second

— absorb short spikes (less than 5 minutes) entirely in the queue without scaling out. Pod startup latency during a 3-minute spike often means new pods arrive after the peak has passed, wasting the startup cost.
Scale-down aggressiveness
— after a burst, scale in promptly. Each idle pod costs money; a 60-second scale-down cooldown strikes the balance between cost and thrash prevention.
Summary
Characterise bursts by rise time, peak duration, and peak-to-baseline ratio before choosing a strategy.
Pre-warm a standby pod pool for predictable spikes; use scheduled HPA patching for known batch windows.
Horizontal scaling is almost always correct for stateless agent workers; vertical scaling helps for memory or IPC bottlenecks.
Drive the autoscaler from queue depth and P95 latency, not CPU or memory, for AI workloads.
Apply zero cooldown on scale-up and a 60-second stabilisation window on scale-down to prevent both latency spikes and oscillation.
Model burst cost in two components (compute + token overage) and mitigate token overage with pre-purchased burst capacity and model tiering.
